news_summary: Today’s human rights progress includes enhanced mental health support, legal protections for students seeking overdose help, a historic UN resolution against the slave trade, and ongoing resistance to gender oppression under the Taliban.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées enregistrées aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme, on peut citer le renforcement de l’aide en santé mentale, les protections juridiques accordées aux étudiants qui sollicitent une aide en cas de surdose, une résolution historique de l’ONU contre la traite des esclaves, ainsi que la résistance qui se poursuit contre l’oppression liée au genre sous le régime des talibans.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos incluyen un mayor apoyo a la salud mental, protecciones legales para los estudiantes que buscan ayuda por sobredosis, una resolución histórica de la ONU contra la trata de esclavos y la resistencia continua contra la opresión de género bajo el régimen talibán.

articles.title: US: Rights-Respecting Approaches to Mental Health Crisis
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Two Telecare San Diego Mobile Crisis Response Team members, Monique Harris, licensed clinician, and Ann Huynh, case manager, provide crisis support to a person in Chicano Park, San Diego, California, 2023. 
                    © 2023 MCRT
          
    


  
(New York) – Communities throughout the country are developing alternative models of mental health crisis response Human Rights Watch, New York Lawyers for the Public Interest, and the Center for Racial and Disability Justice at UCLA Law School said in a report released today. These approaches are desperately needed as US police kill hundreds of people each year, many of whom had documented mental health conditions, and as federal, state, and local jurisdictions seek to implement increasingly coercive approaches to mental health crisis response and treatment. The 66-page report, “‘Self-Determination is the Pathway to Liberation’: Alternative Mental Health Crisis Response in the United States,” identifies key elements of rights-respecting responses to mental health crises and explores how alternative mental health crisis response programs have carried out these approaches in practice. Many of these models share core elements, including promoting individual autonomy, providing voluntary support rather than mandating compliance, and avoiding unnecessary law enforcement involvement. “Having police as the primary or default responders to people experiencing mental health crises is ineffective and sometimes lethal, given their orientation toward force and compliance,” said Tanya Greene, US program director at Human Rights Watch. “Fortunately, there are alternate approaches that emphasize personal autonomy and consent to treatment.”Researchers studied over 150 crisis response programs from across the United States and feature eight that have committed to implement key aspects of supportive, rights-based mental health crisis response without police as primary or default responders. The researchers interviewed program administrators and, where possible, community members and advocates for unaffiliated perspectives on the programs. Researchers used international human rights law and standards as a tool for identifying key elements of rights-respecting programs. In many cases, police presence escalates mental health crises and results in coercion and violence. People with mental health conditions are particularly at risk to police violence and are much more likely to be killed during police encounters than people without a disability. This risk is especially true for Black people and other people of color with mental health conditions. Police often tend to approach people in crisis with commands and calls for compliance in situations when a more nuanced and supportive interaction that peers—those with lived mental health experience—and mental health workers can provide is needed. The eight programs featured in the report are based in communities around the United States. Some are connected to local government, while others operate independently. Their response teams differ in composition, though most include peers, social workers, emergency medical technicians or paramedics, and crisis intervention specialists. They typically respond on-site to the person experiencing a crisis, and provide a variety of services, including assessments, de-escalation, safety planning, crisis counseling, education, transportation, referrals to community resources, and follow-ups. The programs were developed with the recognition that police-centered mental health crisis responses have often led to violence and harm to the person in need of support. Black people and other people of color have been especially exposed to that violence and harm, due in part to existing structural racism in policing, mental health care, and more generally throughout society. Programs that emphasize non-coercive models and non-police responses seek to avoid that violence and provide more effective support for people experiencing crises by mobilizing and training peers and other professionals, steeped in the culture and communities they serve.The metrics by which the programs were evaluated include eliminating police as primary or default responders, avoiding involuntary treatment, implementing a consent-centered approach to treatment, promoting participation of peers, providing trauma-informed and culturally responsive training, and maintaining a deep connection to the communities they serve. Researchers evaluated the programs’ commitment to providing accessible services, response times comparable with other emergency services, follow-up care, and minimization of power imbalances between service providers and those they support. “These programs serve as examples for how we can truly serve individuals and communities to make their own decisions through support and care,” said William Juhn, senior staff attorney of the Disability Justice Program at New York Lawyers for the Public Interest.While no one program purports to embrace or implement all these rights-respecting criteria, each program is oriented around at least some of them. Researchers did not evaluate outcomes and did not endorse any program’s particular model or approach to crisis response.“As federal, state, and local governments move toward more coercive approaches to mental health crisis response, like involuntary commitments, hospitalizations, and forced medication, it is important to understand that programs honoring human rights do exist,” said Jordyn Jensen, community engagement and communications manager at the Center for Racial and Disability Justice.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Two Telecare San Diego Mobile Crisis Response Team members, Monique Harris, licensed clinician, and Ann Huynh, case manager, provide crisis support to a person in Chicano Park, San Diego, California, 2023. © 2023 MCRT

(New York) – Communities throughout the country are developing alternative models of mental health crisis response Human Rights Watch, New York Lawyers for the Public Interest, and the Center for Racial and Disability Justice at UCLA Law School said in a report released today. These approaches are desperately needed as US police kill hundreds of people each year, many of whom had documented mental health conditions, and as federal, state, and local jurisdictions seek to implement increasingly coercive approaches to mental health crisis response and treatment.

The 66-page report, “‘Self-Determination is the Pathway to Liberation’: Alternative Mental Health Crisis Response in the United States,” identifies key elements of rights-respecting responses to mental health crises and explores how alternative mental health crisis response programs have carried out these approaches in practice. Many of these models share core elements, including promoting individual autonomy, providing voluntary support rather than mandating compliance, and avoiding unnecessary law enforcement involvement.

“Having police as the primary or default responders to people experiencing mental health crises is ineffective and sometimes lethal, given their orientation toward force and compliance,” said Tanya Greene, US program director at Human Rights Watch. “Fortunately, there are alternate approaches that emphasize personal autonomy and consent to treatment.”

Researchers studied over 150 crisis response programs from across the United States and feature eight that have committed to implement key aspects of supportive, rights-based mental health crisis response without police as primary or default responders. The researchers interviewed program administrators and, where possible, community members and advocates for unaffiliated perspectives on the programs. Researchers used international human rights law and standards as a tool for identifying key elements of rights-respecting programs.

In many cases, police presence escalates mental health crises and results in coercion and violence. People with mental health conditions are particularly at risk to police violence and are much more likely to be killed during police encounters than people without a disability. This risk is especially true for Black people and other people of color with mental health conditions. Police often tend to approach people in crisis with commands and calls for compliance in situations when a more nuanced and supportive interaction that peers—those with lived mental health experience—and mental health workers can provide is needed.

The eight programs featured in the report are based in communities around the United States. Some are connected to local government, while others operate independently. Their response teams differ in composition, though most include peers, social workers, emergency medical technicians or paramedics, and crisis intervention specialists. They typically respond on-site to the person experiencing a crisis, and provide a variety of services, including assessments, de-escalation, safety planning, crisis counseling, education, transportation, referrals to community resources, and follow-ups.

The programs were developed with the recognition that police-centered mental health crisis responses have often led to violence and harm to the person in need of support. Black people and other people of color have been especially exposed to that violence and harm, due in part to existing structural racism in policing, mental health care, and more generally throughout society.

Programs that emphasize non-coercive models and non-police responses seek to avoid that violence and provide more effective support for people experiencing crises by mobilizing and training peers and other professionals, steeped in the culture and communities they serve.

The metrics by which the programs were evaluated include eliminating police as primary or default responders, avoiding involuntary treatment, implementing a consent-centered approach to treatment, promoting participation of peers, providing trauma-informed and culturally responsive training, and maintaining a deep connection to the communities they serve. Researchers evaluated the programs’ commitment to providing accessible services, response times comparable with other emergency services, follow-up care, and minimization of power imbalances between service providers and those they support.

“These programs serve as examples for how we can truly serve individuals and communities to make their own decisions through support and care,” said William Juhn, senior staff attorney of the Disability Justice Program at New York Lawyers for the Public Interest.

While no one program purports to embrace or implement all these rights-respecting criteria, each program is oriented around at least some of them. Researchers did not evaluate outcomes and did not endorse any program’s particular model or approach to crisis response.

“As federal, state, and local governments move toward more coercive approaches to mental health crisis response, like involuntary commitments, hospitalizations, and forced medication, it is important to understand that programs honoring human rights do exist,” said Jordyn Jensen, community engagement and communications manager at the Center for Racial and Disability Justice.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the development and implementation of alternative, rights-respecting mental health crisis response programs across the US that avoid police involvement, reduce violence, and promote autonomy and consent. These programs address a significant public safety and human rights issue with broad societal impact, especially for marginalized communities, and provide detailed context on their approaches and principles.
articles.personality_title: New mental health crisis programs reduce police involvement and promote personal choice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, police often respond to mental health crises, but this approach has sometimes led to violence, especially affecting people with mental health conditions and communities of color.

**What happened** – A new report by Human Rights Watch, UCLA Law School, and New York Lawyers for the Public Interest highlights alternative mental health crisis response programs across the country. These programs avoid using police as the main responders and instead send teams made up of trained peers, social workers, and medical professionals to help people in crisis.

**Impact** – These programs focus on respecting individual rights by offering voluntary support, avoiding forced treatment, and working closely with the communities they serve. By not involving police, they reduce the risk of violence and provide help that fits each person’s needs, especially protecting vulnerable groups.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope more communities will adopt these rights-based programs. As some government areas push for stricter mental health laws, these programs show that respectful, non-coercive care is possible and effective.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New mental health crisis response programs in the US offer safer, more respectful help by reducing police involvement and focusing on personal choice and community support.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux programmes de crise en santé mentale réduisent l'intervention policière et favorisent le choix personnel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, la police intervient souvent lors de crises de santé mentale, ce qui a parfois conduit à de la violence, notamment envers les personnes souffrant de troubles mentaux et les communautés de couleur.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un nouveau rapport de Human Rights Watch, de la faculté de droit de l'UCLA et de New York Lawyers for the Public Interest met en lumière des programmes alternatifs de réponse aux crises de santé mentale dans tout le pays. Ces programmes évitent que la police soit l’intervenant principal et envoient plutôt des équipes composées de pairs formés, d’assistants sociaux et de professionnels médicaux pour aider les personnes en crise.

**Impact** – Ces programmes respectent les droits individuels en offrant un soutien volontaire, en évitant les traitements forcés et en travaillant étroitement avec les communautés locales. En n’impliquant pas la police, ils réduisent le risque de violence et apportent une aide adaptée aux besoins de chacun, protégeant particulièrement les groupes vulnérables.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent que davantage de communautés adopteront ces programmes respectueux des droits. Alors que certaines autorités poussent vers des lois plus strictes en santé mentale, ces programmes démontrent qu’un accompagnement respectueux et non coercitif est possible et efficace.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – De nouveaux programmes de réponse aux crises de santé mentale aux États-Unis offrent une aide plus sûre et respectueuse en réduisant l’intervention policière et en mettant l’accent sur le choix personnel et le soutien communautaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos programas de crisis de salud mental reducen la intervención policial y promueven la elección personal
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, la policía suele responder a crisis de salud mental, pero este enfoque a veces ha causado violencia, especialmente hacia personas con problemas mentales y comunidades de color.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo informe de Human Rights Watch, la Facultad de Derecho de UCLA y New York Lawyers for the Public Interest destaca programas alternativos de respuesta a crisis de salud mental en todo el país. Estos programas evitan que la policía sea la principal respuesta y envían equipos formados por pares, trabajadores sociales y profesionales médicos para ayudar a las personas en crisis.

**Impacto** – Estos programas se centran en respetar los derechos individuales ofreciendo apoyo voluntario, evitando tratamientos forzados y trabajando estrechamente con las comunidades. Al no involucrar a la policía, reducen el riesgo de violencia y brindan ayuda que se adapta a las necesidades de cada persona, protegiendo especialmente a los grupos vulnerables.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que más comunidades adopten estos programas basados en derechos. Mientras algunas autoridades buscan leyes más estrictas en salud mental, estos programas muestran que la atención respetuosa y no coercitiva es posible y efectiva.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevos programas de respuesta a crisis de salud mental en EE.UU. ofrecen ayuda más segura y respetuosa al reducir la intervención policial y enfocarse en la elección personal y el apoyo comunitario.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two caring hands gently cradling a glowing, translucent heart-shaped lantern, surrounded by softly illuminated silhouettes of diverse community figures—peers, social workers, and crisis responders—standing in a peaceful urban park setting reminiscent of Chicano Park, rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft pastels.

articles.title: New law shields California college students who seek help after overdosing
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY’S EDITORIAL TEAM When TJ McGee overdosed in his UC Berkeley dorm room two years ago, his roommates hesitated before calling for help. He lay on the floor, pale and seizing, while they weighed the risk: call for help and potentially face university consequences, or wait and hope. That hesitation is exactly […]
The post New law shields California college students who seek help after overdosing first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY’S EDITORIAL TEAM

When TJ McGee overdosed in his UC Berkeley dorm room two years ago, his roommates hesitated before calling for help. He lay on the floor, pale and seizing, while they weighed the risk: call for help and potentially face university consequences, or wait and hope.

That hesitation is exactly the problem Assembly Bill 602 is designed to fix.

Signed into law in October 2025 and taking effect in July 2026, AB 602 requires all campuses in California’s public university systems, both California State University and University of California, to offer students rehabilitation services for drug and alcohol use before taking disciplinary action against them following an overdose.

What makes the law stand out is who drafted it: a group of students from UC Berkeley, UC Davis, and other universities, working alongside Assemblymember Matt Haney of San Francisco.

The fear that keeps students from calling for help

Under existing campus policies, students at schools like UC Berkeley can face disciplinary action, including removal from campus housing, for substance use. Violate the policy more than once, and the university can permanently remove a student from campus entirely.

The result is a system where calling for help after an overdose could trigger consequences that damage or end a student’s academic career.

“In that moment, when someone is overdosing, the stakes are life and death. But for the person on the ground, there is no guarantee that help will mean healing,” McGee told California Assembly members at a committee hearing in April 2025.

After his own overdose, the university placed him on academic probation. Months of recovery followed with no institutional support. “No one asked if he was OK. No one pointed him towards support… he spent the next months crawling his way through recovery alone, piecing together what he could, holding his education together with duct tape and desperation,” he said at a Senate committee hearing in July 2025.

Saanvi Arora, a fourth-year student at UC Berkeley who helped draft the bill, said the fear of consequences is real enough that some students simply opt out of getting help. “They would much rather just see what happens and hope that they’re OK,” she said. “Leave it up to fate, honestly, than call or go downstairs and bring a trusted campus official to help them.”

Students who lived it wrote the bill

Arora and Aditi Hariharan, president of the UC Student Association and a student at UC Davis, both came to it through loss. Arora lost a close friend to an overdose at 15. Hariharan watched a high school friend struggle with alcohol addiction during her first year of college, unable to access campus recovery resources, and eventually drop out.

“You can’t engage in recovery if you’re already dead,” Hariharan said. “This legislation allows folks to seek medical care.”

Haney, who agreed to help the students after they reached out to his office, said the problem was both urgent and solvable. “We should protect students from these hugely harmful academic consequences when they do the right thing and call for help,” he said.

What the law covers and where it draws the line

AB 602 is not a blanket amnesty. Earlier drafts included protections for bystanders who call for help and had no cap on how often students could avoid discipline by opting for rehab. University officials pushed back on both provisions, concerned that the original language could shield students from consequences for unrelated conduct violations.

The final version limits overdose protections to once per academic term and does not extend coverage to students who call for help on behalf of someone else.

Cal State has not yet built a systemwide program to implement the law. “We don’t have a program that’s directly tied to this bill just yet. When it comes to rehabilitation services, that is certainly one of the big questions that we’re still assessing,” said Ray Murillo, Cal State’s interim assistant vice chancellor for student affairs.

Six UC campuses, including Berkeley and Davis, already run collegiate recovery programs. The law builds on that foundation and raises the expectation statewide. Starting this summer, students at California public universities will have a legal right to treatment before punishment. For the students who wrote the bill, that right is the whole point.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a new California law that protects college students who seek help after overdosing by requiring rehabilitation services before disciplinary actions. This law addresses a critical public health and safety issue with broad significance for students statewide, reducing fear of seeking emergency help and promoting recovery over punishment. The story includes detailed context, the legislative process, and the involvement of students who experienced the problem firsthand, demonstrating meaningful positive impact on student welfare and rights.
articles.personality_title: California law protects college students seeking help after overdose
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many college students in California feared calling for help after an overdose because they could face punishment from their universities. This fear sometimes stopped students from getting emergency aid when they needed it most.

**What happened** – In October 2025, California passed Assembly Bill 602 (AB 602), a new law that requires public universities to offer rehabilitation services to students after an overdose before any disciplinary action is taken. The law, starting July 2026, was created with help from students at UC Berkeley, UC Davis, and other campuses, working with Assemblymember Matt Haney.

**Impact** – This law changes how universities handle overdoses by focusing on support and recovery instead of punishment. Before, students risked academic probation or even removal from campus housing, which made them hesitate to call for help. Now, students can get medical care without fearing immediate penalties, which can save lives and improve recovery chances.

**What's next step** – California State University is still planning how to set up rehabilitation programs to follow the law. Meanwhile, six University of California campuses already have recovery programs, and the law will encourage more schools to offer similar support. Starting summer 2026, students will have a legal right to treatment before facing discipline.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new California law protects college students from punishment when they seek help after an overdose by ensuring access to rehabilitation services first.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une loi californienne protège les étudiants qui demandent de l’aide après une overdose
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux étudiants californiens avaient peur d’appeler à l’aide après une overdose, craignant des sanctions de leur université. Cette peur les empêchait parfois de recevoir des soins d’urgence essentiels.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En octobre 2025, la Californie a adopté la loi AB 602, qui oblige les universités publiques à offrir des services de réhabilitation aux étudiants après une overdose avant toute sanction disciplinaire. Cette loi, qui entrera en vigueur en juillet 2026, a été créée avec la participation d’étudiants de l’UC Berkeley, UC Davis et d’autres campus, en collaboration avec le député Matt Haney.

**Impact** – Cette loi modifie la manière dont les universités gèrent les overdoses, en mettant l’accent sur le soutien et la réhabilitation plutôt que sur la punition. Avant, les étudiants risquaient la probation académique ou l’expulsion du logement universitaire, ce qui les empêchait d’appeler à l’aide. Désormais, ils peuvent recevoir des soins médicaux sans craindre de sanctions immédiates, ce qui peut sauver des vies.

**Prochaine étape** – Le système Cal State doit encore organiser ses programmes de réhabilitation pour appliquer la loi. En attendant, six campus de l’Université de Californie ont déjà des programmes de rétablissement, et la loi encouragera d’autres établissements à offrir un soutien similaire. Dès l’été 2026, les étudiants auront un droit légal à un traitement avant toute sanction.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle loi californienne protège les étudiants universitaires contre les sanctions lorsqu’ils demandent de l’aide après une overdose en garantissant l’accès aux services de réhabilitation.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva ley en California protege a estudiantes universitarios que buscan ayuda tras una sobredosis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos estudiantes universitarios en California tenían miedo de pedir ayuda después de una sobredosis porque podían enfrentar sanciones por parte de sus universidades. Este miedo a veces les impedía recibir atención médica urgente.

**Qué pasó** – En octubre de 2025, California aprobó la Ley AB 602, que exige a las universidades públicas ofrecer servicios de rehabilitación a los estudiantes después de una sobredosis antes de tomar medidas disciplinarias. La ley, que comenzará en julio de 2026, fue creada con la ayuda de estudiantes de UC Berkeley, UC Davis y otros campus, junto con el asambleísta Matt Haney.

**Impacto** – Esta ley cambia la forma en que las universidades manejan las sobredosis, enfocándose en el apoyo y la recuperación en lugar del castigo. Antes, los estudiantes podían enfrentar probatoria académica o expulsión de la vivienda, lo que les hacía dudar en pedir ayuda. Ahora, pueden recibir atención médica sin temor a sanciones inmediatas, lo que puede salvar vidas.

**Próximo paso** – El sistema de la Universidad Estatal de California aún está planificando cómo implementar los programas de rehabilitación para cumplir con la ley. Mientras tanto, seis campus de la Universidad de California ya cuentan con programas de recuperación, y la ley alentará a más escuelas a ofrecer apoyo similar. A partir del verano de 2026, los estudiantes tendrán derecho legal a tratamiento antes de cualquier disciplina.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva ley en California protege a los estudiantes universitarios de sanciones cuando buscan ayuda tras una sobredosis, garantizando primero el acceso a servicios de rehabilitación.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a university dorm room softly illuminated by a gentle golden light, where a pair of open hands cradle a glowing heart-shaped symbol wrapped protectively in a bandage, surrounded by subtle outlines of supportive student silhouettes standing nearby, conveying care, hope, and new legal protection for students seeking help after an overdose.

articles.title: Landmark UN Resolution on the Slave Trade
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Formerly enslaved people sit at Foller's House in Cumberland Landing, Virginia, US, circa 1850.
                    © 1850 Fotosearch/Getty Images
          
    


  
Commemorating the International Day of Remembrance of the Victims of Slavery and the Transatlantic Slave Trade, the United Nations General Assembly passed a landmark resolution, introduced by Ghana, on March 25 that seeks to advance reparatory justice for the trafficking of enslaved Africans and the racialized chattel enslavement of Africans.African, Caribbean, and Latin American states delivered powerful speeches at this year’s observance. Ghana’s president, speaking on behalf of the African group, described the initiative as “a route to healing and reparative justice” for enslavement legacies “beyond symbolic acknowledgment and toward institutional accountability.” It reflects longstanding advocacy by African and Caribbean states and civil society.The resolution calls for restitution of looted cultural property, formal apologies, and consideration of compensation and other reparatory measures, framing these as necessary steps to address contemporary inequalities linked to slavery, colonialism, and systemic racism. It describes the trafficking of enslaved Africans and the racialized chattel enslavement of Africans as the “gravest crime against humanity.”The resolution passed with 123 votes in favor. The United States, Israel, and Argentina voted no and 52 states abstained, including the United Kingdom, Canada, and all European Union member states, including Spain.The US delegation’s justifications for its vote against the resolution included claims that the resolution narrowly focused on Western states’ responsibility and suggested that the claims of compensation were “an attempt to reallocate modern resources to people and nations who are distantly related to the historical victims.”Historically, states like the UK chose to compensate former owners of enslaved people—not the enslaved people themselves—based on allegations that they had “lost” property after the abolition of chattel enslavement.The abstentions from the UK and EU member states were expected, even if unjustified, given European states’ role in colonial-era atrocities and their lasting impacts. While calling the slave trade an “unparalleled tragedy,” they, like the US, question the legal basis for reparations, contending that colonial-era atrocities predated modern international law.There is growing recognition of reparations for historical and ongoing injustices, including reports of the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights and the 2001 Durban Declaration and Programme of Action, a comprehensive UN blueprint to combat systemic racism, which explicitly acknowledges slavery and the slave trade as crimes against humanity and calls for remedies and reparatory justice.The vote underscores a continuing divide between Global South countries experiencing enduring consequences of colonial atrocities, enslavement, and the slave trade, and many Global North countries unwilling to take responsibility and to take actions.The legacy of enslavement remains a living structural injustice, with a global majority, including civil society in Europe and US, increasingly calling for meaningful repair rather than symbolic recognition alone.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Formerly enslaved people sit at Foller's House in Cumberland Landing, Virginia, US, circa 1850. © 1850 Fotosearch/Getty Images

Commemorating the International Day of Remembrance of the Victims of Slavery and the Transatlantic Slave Trade, the United Nations General Assembly passed a landmark resolution, introduced by Ghana, on March 25 that seeks to advance reparatory justice for the trafficking of enslaved Africans and the racialized chattel enslavement of Africans.

African, Caribbean, and Latin American states delivered powerful speeches at this year’s observance. Ghana’s president, speaking on behalf of the African group, described the initiative as “a route to healing and reparative justice” for enslavement legacies “beyond symbolic acknowledgment and toward institutional accountability.” It reflects longstanding advocacy by African and Caribbean states and civil society.

The resolution calls for restitution of looted cultural property, formal apologies, and consideration of compensation and other reparatory measures, framing these as necessary steps to address contemporary inequalities linked to slavery, colonialism, and systemic racism. It describes the trafficking of enslaved Africans and the racialized chattel enslavement of Africans as the “gravest crime against humanity.”

The resolution passed with 123 votes in favor. The United States, Israel, and Argentina voted no and 52 states abstained, including the United Kingdom, Canada, and all European Union member states, including Spain.

The US delegation’s justifications for its vote against the resolution included claims that the resolution narrowly focused on Western states’ responsibility and suggested that the claims of compensation were “an attempt to reallocate modern resources to people and nations who are distantly related to the historical victims.”

Historically, states like the UK chose to compensate former owners of enslaved people—not the enslaved people themselves—based on allegations that they had “lost” property after the abolition of chattel enslavement.

The abstentions from the UK and EU member states were expected, even if unjustified, given European states’ role in colonial-era atrocities and their lasting impacts. While calling the slave trade an “unparalleled tragedy,” they, like the US, question the legal basis for reparations, contending that colonial-era atrocities predated modern international law.

There is growing recognition of reparations for historical and ongoing injustices, including reports of the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights and the 2001 Durban Declaration and Programme of Action, a comprehensive UN blueprint to combat systemic racism, which explicitly acknowledges slavery and the slave trade as crimes against humanity and calls for remedies and reparatory justice.

The vote underscores a continuing divide between Global South countries experiencing enduring consequences of colonial atrocities, enslavement, and the slave trade, and many Global North countries unwilling to take responsibility and to take actions.

The legacy of enslavement remains a living structural injustice, with a global majority, including civil society in Europe and US, increasingly calling for meaningful repair rather than symbolic recognition alone.
articles.reasoning: The UN General Assembly passed a landmark resolution advancing reparatory justice for the transatlantic slave trade, calling for restitution, formal apologies, and reparations to address systemic racism and historical injustices. This represents a significant step toward institutional accountability and healing for a grave historical crime with broad societal impact, especially for African, Caribbean, and Latin American nations. Despite some opposition and abstentions, the resolution reflects growing global recognition of reparations and justice for slavery's enduring legacy.
articles.personality_title: UN passes major resolution to address slavery’s lasting impact
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, the world remembers the victims of slavery and the transatlantic slave trade. This trade involved forcibly taking millions of Africans to work as slaves, especially in the Americas. Many countries and communities are still affected by this history today.

**What happened** – On March 25, the United Nations General Assembly approved a new resolution introduced by Ghana. This resolution aims to promote justice for the descendants of enslaved Africans. It calls for returning stolen cultural items, formal apologies, and possibly compensation to help fix inequalities caused by slavery and racism. The resolution passed with 123 votes in favor. Some countries, like the United States, Israel, and Argentina, voted against it, while others, including the UK, Canada, and many European Union nations, chose not to vote either way.

**Impact** – This resolution is important because it goes beyond just saying sorry. It asks countries to take real responsibility for the harms caused by slavery and colonialism. African, Caribbean, and Latin American nations see this as a chance to begin true healing. It also highlights a divide between countries that experienced slavery’s effects directly and those hesitant to accept legal responsibility. The resolution calls slavery “the gravest crime against humanity,” showing the seriousness of the issue.

**What's next step** – The resolution encourages further talks and actions to make reparations possible. It may lead to more formal apologies, returning cultural treasures, and discussions about financial compensation. The goal is to reduce ongoing inequalities linked to this history. Civil society groups around the world are expected to continue pushing governments to act beyond symbolic gestures.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new resolution marks a key step toward justice and accountability for the long-lasting effects of the transatlantic slave trade.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU adopte une résolution majeure pour réparer les conséquences de l’esclavage
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, le monde se souvient des victimes de l’esclavage et de la traite transatlantique des esclaves. Cette traite a forcé des millions d’Africains à travailler comme esclaves, surtout dans les Amériques. De nombreux pays et communautés sont encore touchés par cette histoire aujourd’hui.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 25 mars, l’Assemblée générale des Nations Unies a adopté une nouvelle résolution présentée par le Ghana. Cette résolution vise à promouvoir la justice pour les descendants des Africains réduits en esclavage. Elle demande la restitution des biens culturels volés, des excuses officielles, et éventuellement des compensations pour aider à réparer les inégalités causées par l’esclavage et le racisme. La résolution a été adoptée avec 123 votes pour. Certains pays, comme les États-Unis, Israël et l’Argentine, ont voté contre, tandis que d’autres, dont le Royaume-Uni, le Canada et plusieurs pays de l’Union européenne, se sont abstenus.

**Impact** – Cette résolution est importante car elle va au-delà d’un simple pardon. Elle demande aux pays de prendre une réelle responsabilité pour les dommages causés par l’esclavage et le colonialisme. Les nations africaines, caribéennes et latino-américaines y voient une opportunité de commencer une véritable guérison. Elle souligne aussi un clivage entre les pays directement touchés par l’esclavage et ceux qui hésitent à assumer une responsabilité juridique. La résolution qualifie l’esclavage de « crime le plus grave contre l’humanité », montrant ainsi la gravité du sujet.

**Prochaine étape** – La résolution encourage des discussions et actions supplémentaires pour rendre possibles les réparations. Cela pourrait entraîner davantage d’excuses officielles, la restitution d’objets culturels et des discussions sur des compensations financières. L’objectif est de réduire les inégalités persistantes liées à cette histoire. Les groupes de la société civile du monde entier devraient continuer à pousser les gouvernements à aller au-delà des gestes symboliques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle résolution de l’ONU marque une étape importante vers la justice et la responsabilité face aux effets durables de la traite transatlantique des esclaves.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU aprueba una resolución clave para reparar el legado de la esclavitud
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, el mundo recuerda a las víctimas de la esclavitud y la trata transatlántica de esclavos. Esta trata forzó a millones de africanos a trabajar como esclavos, especialmente en América. Muchos países y comunidades todavía sufren las consecuencias de esta historia.

**Qué pasó** – El 25 de marzo, la Asamblea General de las Naciones Unidas aprobó una resolución presentada por Ghana. Esta resolución busca promover la justicia para los descendientes de africanos esclavizados. Pide devolver bienes culturales robados, disculpas formales y considerar compensaciones para ayudar a corregir desigualdades causadas por la esclavitud y el racismo. La resolución fue aprobada con 123 votos a favor. Algunos países, como Estados Unidos, Israel y Argentina, votaron en contra, mientras que otros, incluyendo Reino Unido, Canadá y varios países de la Unión Europea, se abstuvieron.

**Impacto** – Esta resolución es importante porque va más allá de solo pedir perdón. Pide a los países que asuman una responsabilidad real por los daños causados por la esclavitud y el colonialismo. Las naciones africanas, caribeñas y latinoamericanas la ven como una oportunidad para comenzar una verdadera sanación. También muestra una división entre países que sufrieron directamente la esclavitud y aquellos que dudan en aceptar responsabilidad legal. La resolución califica la esclavitud como “el crimen más grave contra la humanidad”, mostrando la seriedad del tema.

**Próximo paso** – La resolución fomenta más conversaciones y acciones para hacer posibles las reparaciones. Esto puede llevar a más disculpas formales, la devolución de tesoros culturales y discusiones sobre compensaciones económicas. El objetivo es reducir las desigualdades actuales vinculadas a esta historia. Se espera que grupos de la sociedad civil en todo el mundo sigan presionando a los gobiernos para actuar más allá de gestos simbólicos.

**Resumen en una frase** – La nueva resolución de la ONU marca un paso clave hacia la justicia y responsabilidad por los efectos duraderos de la trata transatlántica de esclavos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, ancient African wooden ship’s hull gently merging into a vibrant, golden sunrise over calm waters, with chains dissolving into blooming flowers and intertwined roots symbolizing healing and reparative justice, using natural earth tones and soft light to evoke hope and reconciliation.

articles.title: Our generation will continue resisting the Taliban’s restrictions on women and girls
articles.summary: The years 2020 and 2021 were among the most accomplished years of my life, because I had found my path and was on the verge of achieving all my goals.
articles.content:
This article was written by Fareshtah in 2025. It is published with her consent under the special coverage that tells the stories of young women and girls from Afghanistan following the Taliban takeover in August 2021.

I am well familiar with the term “war between the Taliban and the government” because I have witnessed the trade-offs and conflicts between the two sides since my childhood. Our fate has always fluctuated because of their actions and decisions.

I was born in the last year of the first Taliban regime (1996–2001) in Afghanistan’s central Ghor Province. Fortunately, I had gained the right to education after the fall of the first regime. I still remember the excitement of my first day at school, donning a black uniform and white scarf. It was a special feeling!

I had not yet entered the university when we heard news of the peace talks between the U.S. and Taliban in February 2020, and we had hoped for peace and a better future.

Finally, after passing the entrance exam, I entered the university. The years 2020 and 2021 were among the most accomplished years of my life, because I had found my path and was on the verge of achieving all my goals.

I was studying Sharia and Islamic Sciences and wanted to become a successful attorney, so I registered for the two-year legal skills program through the Asia Foundation and the Ministry of Higher Education.

At the same time, the war between the Taliban and government forces was intensifying. The security situation was worsening, and because of the extreme heat waves, the university and the legal skills classes were held both in person and online.

I saw news of the collapse of the provinces one after another, but I was still thinking about my goals and pursuing them. On August 13, 2021, the war had reached the gates of our home city of Herat. The next day, I was trying to connect to my online class in my room when my brother opened the door and said, “Put it down, it’s over.”

The end of dreams

With the fall of Herat, there was no exam, no presentation, and no news of the class or its continuation. All my hopes suddenly evaporated before my eyes, as if I had been in a very sweet dream and someone had woken me up with a heavy blow.

Such a heavy blow that it took away my power of speech and tears. I felt that my soul had been separated from my body, and all that was left was a lifeless body.

My heart burned with the desire to continue university and the legal skills class, but with complete despair, I could do nothing but cry.

After a few months, private universities opened, but there was no news of public universities opening. Six months later, public universities finally opened. I studied intensively for the last three semesters and finished my thesis. I was supposed to defend my thesis on a Saturday (December 24) and attend my batch’s graduation ceremony on the Monday (December 26).

However, on Tuesday, December 20, 2022, a decree was issued prohibiting girls from attending universities.

I contacted my professor, and he said, “Come on, defend your thesis because you’ve graduated.”

On Saturday morning, I left for the university, happy that I had been given the right to defend my thesis, yet also sad that so many other girls had dropped out.

When I arrived at the university gate, a Taliban member blocked the gate and refused to let me out of the rickshaw, which I had taken to get there.

Finally, I got down and hurried toward the university gate, but he stood in front of me with the gun.

I ignored him and approached the gate. He grabbed the strap of my laptop bag, pulled it, and said violently: “You don't understand what I’m talking about?! Or should I blow your brains out?!”

He fired a shot in the air, and a strange and unpleasant ringing filled my ears.

A bystander came toward me and said, “Sister, please go.” He was one of the university guards I knew. I left and went to the Shame Danesh Publishing House. As soon as I entered, my anger exploded, and tears flew down my face. The tears in my eyes had become like a sea that could not be dried.

I no longer cared whether people saw me crying as I left that place. I was walking home in tears when I heard my cell phone ring.

It was my father, and he asked: “Have you finished your defense, and how was it?” Crying, and in one breath, I told him the story. He comforted me, told me about the hardships and tortures he had endured, and advised me to be patient and persevere.

From one set of goals to another

I got home, slept a little, then started searching for online courses and educational opportunities, but since I couldn’t find a suitable opportunity, I started reading books.

I participated in a women’s reading group twice a week. We read different books, but this time I really needed a motivational and psychological book.

I visited the group and saw the name of the book, “Wuthering Heights.” Reading it gave me the motivation to start again and helped me renew myself. Nearly a year after finishing my bachelor’s degree, I managed to defend my thesis online.

But I had no more enthusiasm, because my goals had completely deviated from their original path. I had to set new goals. I started studying, researching, and participating in online programs.

I started my computer and English courses online, but after a few months, they were canceled again due to Taliban restrictions, and there has been no news of them since.

A decree was issued allowing girls to attend educational courses, and I also registered for an in-person course, but they quickly reneged on the promise and banned it again.

Over the last three years, I have participated in various online programs, trainings, and conferences because my dream job is not available to me at the moment.

I realized that the root of all these problems is a lack of awareness. I have been teaching in online schools for girls who have been deprived of education for almost two years, and I have also started teaching Islamic culture courses at the university.

I want to fight ignorance and injustice in my own way. Whether it is by transferring my little knowledge by reading a line from a book, or writing, or even tying a root to hope and growing its withered buds in my heart and that of my fellow human beings.

I hope for a better tomorrow and a bright future for my country. Our generation that suffered this injustice and ignorance will never give birth to a child who will surrender to the oppressor and the ignorant. They will not allow a third repetition in history with such a bitter experience.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring resilience and determination of Afghan women and girls resisting Taliban restrictions on education and rights. It reports real-world impact by showcasing ongoing efforts to pursue education and fight ignorance despite oppressive conditions. The story has broad significance as it reflects the struggle for women's rights and education in Afghanistan, affecting many. It contains substantial context about the challenges faced and the personal journey of the author, making it a focused and meaningful narrative of hope and resistance.
articles.personality_title: Afghan woman fights to keep education alive despite Taliban bans
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – After the Taliban took control of Afghanistan in August 2021, many restrictions were placed on women's rights, especially their access to education. This changed the lives of many young women who had just started pursuing their dreams.

**What happened** – Fareshtah, born during the first Taliban rule, had worked hard to study law and Islamic sciences. She was about to graduate from university when the Taliban banned girls from attending public universities in late 2022. Despite threats and being stopped by armed guards, she defended her thesis online. She also started teaching other girls through online schools and kept learning new skills even as courses were canceled.

**Impact** – Fareshtah’s story shows how Afghan women refuse to give up on education, even when facing danger and unfair rules. Her efforts to teach others and continue learning help fight ignorance and keep hope alive for many girls who are denied schooling. This personal journey highlights the ongoing struggle for women’s rights in Afghanistan.

**What's next step** – Fareshtah plans to keep teaching and learning online while waiting for opportunities to return to in-person education. She hopes her generation will inspire future Afghan girls to resist oppression and build a better future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Despite harsh Taliban bans, Afghan women like Fareshtah continue to fight for education and inspire others to do the same.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une femme afghane lutte pour maintenir l'éducation malgré les interdictions des talibans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Après la prise de contrôle de l'Afghanistan par les talibans en août 2021, de nombreuses restrictions ont été imposées aux droits des femmes, notamment leur accès à l'éducation. Cela a profondément changé la vie de nombreuses jeunes femmes qui venaient de commencer à poursuivre leurs rêves.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Fareshtah, née pendant le premier régime taliban, avait travaillé dur pour étudier le droit et les sciences islamiques. Elle était sur le point d'obtenir son diplôme universitaire lorsque les talibans ont interdit aux filles de fréquenter les universités publiques fin 2022. Malgré les menaces et les contrôles par des gardes armés, elle a soutenu sa thèse en ligne. Elle a aussi commencé à enseigner à d'autres filles via des écoles en ligne et a continué à apprendre de nouvelles compétences même lorsque des cours ont été annulés.

**Impact** – L'histoire de Fareshtah montre comment les femmes afghanes refusent d'abandonner l'éducation, même face au danger et à des règles injustes. Ses efforts pour enseigner aux autres et continuer à apprendre aident à combattre l'ignorance et à garder l'espoir vivant pour beaucoup de filles privées de scolarité. Ce parcours personnel met en lumière la lutte continue pour les droits des femmes en Afghanistan.

**Prochaine étape** – Fareshtah prévoit de continuer à enseigner et apprendre en ligne en attendant des opportunités de retourner à l'éducation en présentiel. Elle espère que sa génération inspirera les futures filles afghanes à résister à l'oppression et à construire un avenir meilleur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Malgré les interdictions sévères des talibans, des femmes afghanes comme Fareshtah continuent de se battre pour l'éducation et inspirent les autres à faire de même.
articles.personality_title_es: Una mujer afgana lucha por mantener la educación a pesar de las prohibiciones talibanes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Tras la toma de control de Afganistán por los talibanes en agosto de 2021, se impusieron muchas restricciones a los derechos de las mujeres, especialmente a su acceso a la educación. Esto cambió profundamente la vida de muchas jóvenes que acababan de comenzar a perseguir sus sueños.

**Qué pasó** – Fareshtah, nacida durante el primer régimen talibán, trabajó duro para estudiar derecho y ciencias islámicas. Estaba a punto de graduarse cuando los talibanes prohibieron a las niñas asistir a universidades públicas a finales de 2022. A pesar de amenazas y ser detenida por guardias armados, defendió su tesis en línea. También comenzó a enseñar a otras niñas en escuelas en línea y siguió aprendiendo nuevas habilidades aunque cancelaron cursos.

**Impacto** – La historia de Fareshtah muestra cómo las mujeres afganas se niegan a renunciar a la educación, incluso frente al peligro y reglas injustas. Sus esfuerzos por enseñar y seguir aprendiendo ayudan a combatir la ignorancia y mantienen viva la esperanza para muchas niñas sin acceso a la escuela. Este recorrido personal destaca la lucha continua por los derechos de las mujeres en Afganistán.

**Próximo paso** – Fareshtah planea seguir enseñando y aprendiendo en línea mientras espera oportunidades para regresar a la educación presencial. Espera que su generación inspire a futuras niñas afganas a resistir la opresión y construir un futuro mejor.

**Resumen en una frase** – A pesar de las duras prohibiciones talibanes, mujeres afganas como Fareshtah siguen luchando por la educación e inspiran a otras a hacer lo mismo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a young woman’s silhouette sitting at a wooden desk by a window, surrounded by open books and a glowing laptop, with a barred university gate and a shadowy armed figure outside, while soft rays of light gently break through the bars symbolizing hope and resilience amid restriction, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.
